title: NATO Summit Floods Defense Industry With Announcements — From ATACMS in Germany to F-35s for Turkey
subhead: The Ankara NATO summit delivered a cascade of procurement and policy decisions: the US will lift sanctions on Turkey and potentially restore F-35 sales, NATO picked Saab over Boeing for its next AWACS, Lockheed and Rheinmetall will co-produce ATACMS in Germany, and eight allies launched a mega-satellite constellation. Meanwhile, the GAO reported Pentagon weapons programs are 12 years behind schedule, and China tested a ballistic missile that rattled the South Pacific nuclear-free zone.

sections.heading: US to Lift Turkey Sanctions, Open Door to F-35 Sale
sections.body:
President Trump announced during his visit to Turkey for the NATO summit that Washington would lift CAATSA sanctions imposed on Turkey in 2020 over Ankara's purchase of the Russian S-400 air defense system. He also expressed willingness to sell F-35 stealth fighters to Turkey, though significant legal hurdles remain — Congress passed a law prohibiting F-35 sales to any country operating the S-400, citing the risk that the Russian system could collect intelligence on the fighter's radar signature.

According to Reuters, one solution gaining traction would involve Turkey transferring its S-400 systems to a third country, though no agreement has been reached and it remains unclear whether Russia would accept such a move given its end-user obligations. Turkey was originally a partner in the F-35 program and a component manufacturer before its 2019 expulsion.

The announcement marks a dramatic reversal in a dispute that has poisoned US-Turkey defense relations for over half a decade. If the F-35 sale moves forward, it would restore Turkey to the program it was forced out of and reshape NATO's southern flank air power — but the S-400 disposition question remains unresolved.
sections.whyItMatters: The F-35 question is the single biggest unresolved defense procurement issue between the US and a NATO ally. Restoring Turkey to the program would mean reconfiguring supply chains and production lots, while the S-400 workaround could set a precedent for other US partners operating Russian hardware.

sections.heading: NATO Picks Saab GlobalEye Over Boeing E-7 for Next AWACS
sections.body:
NATO formally selected Saab's GlobalEye aircraft over Boeing's E-7 Wedgetail to replace its aging E-3 AWACS fleet, with an initial order of up to 10 aircraft valued at roughly $4.5 billion, according to Saab CEO Micael Johansson. Eleven nations — Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Germany, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Norway, Romania, and Sweden — are joining the procurement.

The decision is a blow to Boeing and to the E-7 program, which has struggled with inconsistent US government support. According to Teal Group analyst J.J. Gertler, NATO's choice was heavily influenced by the Pentagon's initial decision to request no E-7 funding in the FY27 budget — an 'own goal' that signaled to allies that the US was not fully committed to the platform. NATO had originally announced plans to buy six E-7s in 2023, then saw the Trump administration withdraw from the deal in July 2025.

Defense Secretary Hegseth reversed course on E-7 funding in May 2026, and House appropriators backed $1.5 billion in development funding in June. But the damage was already done — NATO had already pivoted toward Saab. The GlobalEye uses sensors on a Bombardier Global 6500 airframe to detect drones, ballistic missiles, and hypersonic threats.
sections.whyItMatters: This is a landmark NATO procurement decision with lasting industrial consequences. Saab now has the flagship NATO AWACS order that Boeing lost, potentially reshaping the European airborne early warning market. The episode also demonstrates how inconsistent US defense budgeting can undermine American contractors in allied competitions.

sections.heading: ATACMS to Be Produced in Germany — First Non-US Manufacturing Site
sections.body:
Lockheed Martin and Rheinmetall signed a memorandum of understanding at the NATO Summit Defence Industry Forum to co-produce the Army Tactical Missile System (ATACMS) at Rheinmetall's Unterlüß site in northern Germany — the first time the ballistic missile will be manufactured outside the United States.

Rheinmetall CEO Armin Papperger called the deal a way to strengthen Germany's 'industrial base for modern defense systems,' while Lockheed's European chief Dennis Goege framed it as pairing 'proven US technology with European manufacturing strength.' Rheinmetall estimates annual European and Ukrainian demand for 600 to 800 ATACMS units, with full production at Unterlüß starting in 2027 and scaling through 2028–2029.

The deal addresses a real bottleneck: Lockheed Martin has been winding down ATACMS production at its Camden, Arkansas facility to prioritize the newer Precision Strike Missile (PrSM). The Underlüß site already has a new artillery-ammunition plant and a rocket-motor factory nearing completion. However, the MOU still requires US government sign-off for ATACMS technology transfer.
sections.whyItMatters: European ATACMS production addresses a critical munitions shortfall exposed by the Ukraine war and the Iran conflict. It also represents a significant technology transfer and industrial deepening for European defense manufacturing — a pattern that is accelerating across multiple weapons categories.

sections.heading: GAO: Pentagon's Top Weapons Programs 12 Years Behind Schedule
sections.body:
The Government Accountability Office's 2026 annual report on major defense acquisition programs found that the Pentagon's 104 most expensive weapons programs face an average delivery timeline of over 12 years, with schedule delays persisting across the board. The programs represent a planned investment of over $2.4 trillion.

GAO found that 46 of 72 programs reported cost increases totaling $122 billion, while only 16 reported decreases totaling $47 billion. The report singled out the middle tier of acquisition pathway — intended to fast-track weapons within five years — as particularly troubled: 18 of 40 programs entered the pathway with immature technologies between 2018 and 2025, including the Hypersonic Attack Cruise Missile and the Next Generation Persistent Overhead Infrared sensor. GAO director Shelby Oakley noted that programs are 'increasingly using the MTA pathway to mature technologies, when the intention of the pathway is to prototype and field within two to five years.'

Specific problem programs include the Navy's MQ-25 Stingray carrier drone (2.5-year delay to initial operational capability), the DDG(X) destroyer (which lacks an acquisition strategy, with the business case 'not apparent'), and the B-52 radar modernization (entering production with 'very little development flight testing completed').
sections.whyItMatters: The GAO report underscores a systemic problem: the Pentagon's acquisition reform efforts are not delivering faster fielding. The gap between the urgency created by the Iran war and great-power competition and the actual pace of weapons delivery continues to widen.

sections.heading: China Tests Submarine-Launched Ballistic Missile Over South Pacific
sections.body:
China's People's Liberation Army Navy tested a submarine-launched ballistic missile that flew approximately 7,000 km (4,350 miles) over the Pacific before splashing down near the island nations of Nauru and Tuvalu, according to a map released by Taiwan's National Security Council and reported by CNN.

Both Nauru and Tuvalu are parties to the South Pacific Nuclear Weapons Free Zone, established by the 1986 Treaty of Rarotonga. While the missile carried a dummy warhead and does not technically violate the treaty's legal text, Pacific nations condemned the test as violating the spirit of the zone. New Zealand's foreign minister said the test 'goes against the object and intent' of Rarotonga. Australia raised similar concerns.

Analyst Olamide Samuel of the Open Nuclear Network in Vienna noted a pattern: 'We have seen a growing trend of nuclear weapons-armed states exerting pressure on nuclear weapons free zones for their geostrategic interests,' citing US pressure on the UK over Diego Garcia and Russia pushing Kazakhstan to allow missile testing alongside China's Pacific test. China last fired a ballistic missile into the Pacific in September 2024.
sections.whyItMatters: The test demonstrates China's growing submarine-based nuclear reach and its willingness to project power into the South Pacific. It also undermines China's own diplomatic position as a defender of nuclear weapons free zones — a contradiction that Pacific island nations are increasingly vocal about.

sections.heading: Pentagon Creates Unmanned Systems Czar, NATO Announces Triton and HALO Constellation
sections.body:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth established the Direct Reporting Portfolio Manager for Unmanned Offensive and Defensive Systems (DRPM-UxS), consolidating all Pentagon drone and counter-drone efforts under a single director who reports to the deputy defense secretary. The memo, dated June 29 and released July 1, places JIATF-401 and the Defense Autonomous Warfare Group under the new organization, with DIU as the primary liaison with commercial companies. The move mirrors the Air Force's earlier consolidation of critical weapons programs under a direct-reporting manager.

At the NATO summit in Ankara, Secretary-General Rutte announced that NATO will acquire up to five MQ-4C Triton high-altitude long-endurance drones from Northrop Grumman for maritime surveillance, and eight allies — Denmark, Canada, Finland, Germany, Norway, the Netherlands, Sweden, and Turkey — launched the HALO (Hybrid Alliance Layered Operations in Space) initiative to network nationally owned military satellites into a mega-constellation for communications, intelligence, and missile tracking. Canada also became the 15th member of NATO's STARLIFT space launch initiative, and Spain joined the Alliance Persistent Surveillance from Space program.

Meanwhile, the US Army tested an autonomous version of its Volcano mine-laying system at Camp Grayling, Michigan, successfully firing mine canisters remotely and autonomously emplacing two minefields — the first live-fire demonstration of the unmanned variant, developed jointly with the UK and contractor Forterra.
sections.whyItMatters: The Pentagon's drone reorganization and NATO's procurement announcements signal institutional urgency around unmanned and space systems. The consolidation of drone oversight aims to fix fragmented acquisition that has slowed fielding. NATO's HALO constellation and Triton purchase represent concrete steps toward alliance-wide ISR integration — a long-stated goal that has lacked delivery.

sections.heading: Iran War Supplemental Deepens FY27 Budget Complexity; Thales Buys Exail for $4.5B
sections.body:
The White House submitted an $87.6 billion supplemental funding request to cover Iran war costs and other expenses, complicating an already complex FY27 defense budget process that now has three components: a $1.1 trillion base request, a $350 billion reconciliation request, and the new supplemental. Of the total, $67.1 billion (77%) goes to the Pentagon. The single largest line item is $21 billion for munitions to rebuild stockpiles depleted by the Iran conflict — on top of the $76.3 billion already in the FY27 base request for munitions, bringing the combined total to $97.3 billion, more than three times the Army's entire FY26 procurement budget.

The supplemental also includes $17.3 billion for operational costs and $2.4 billion for drones, including replacement of at least two dozen MQ-9A Reapers lost in combat. But the Air Force faces a capability gap: the MQ-9A is no longer in production, and a follow-on system is not yet ready.

In defense industry consolidation, Thales agreed to acquire French underwater drone maker Exail Technologies for an implied enterprise value of €3.9 billion ($4.5 billion), outbidding Safran's competing offer. Thales will pay €134 per share — a 44% premium over Exail's pre-Safran-announcement price. Exail provides maritime drones for several European mine-hunting programs, and its acquisition gives Thales critical mass in unmanned underwater warfare and inertial navigation. Meanwhile, Italian shipbuilder Fincantieri announced €600 million in acquisitions of four maritime drone and undersea engineering firms.
sections.whyItMatters: The $97.3 billion combined munitions ask is a staggering figure that reflects the severity of stockpile depletion from the Iran conflict and signals sustained demand for defense contractors. The Thales-Exail and Fincantieri deals show European defense consolidation accelerating in the underwater domain, where demand for autonomous mine-hunting and anti-submarine systems is forecast to grow eightfold by 2030.

bottomLine: The NATO summit in Ankara served as a catalyst for a wave of defense procurement decisions that had been building for months: Saab won the alliance's AWACS replacement, Lockheed and Rheinmetall will build ATACMS in Europe, Turkey is back in the F-35 conversation, and eight nations committed to networking their military satellites. But the day's other stories — 12-year average delays on Pentagon programs, $97 billion in combined munitions requests, and China testing a submarine-launched missile over a nuclear-free zone — underscore the gap between the urgency of the threat environment and the speed at which defense systems are actually delivered.
sources: Defense News, Defense One, Reuters, GAO, NATO, Saab, Thales, Lockheed Martin, Rheinmetall, CNN, Forecast International, La Lettre, Teal Group, Open Nuclear Network, Taiwan National Security Council, DARPA, Picatinny Arsenal, US Navy, White House OMB, US House Appropriations Committee
